title: Heatwave affects north, central and west India as IMD issues red and orange alerts - The News Mill
full_text:
Several states in Northern, Central, and Western India are enduring a severe heatwave, prompting regional authorities to implement emergency measures, according to the India Meteorological Department (IMD).
The IMD defines heatwaves as periods of unusually high temperatures relative to the regional norms, with several areas experiencing temperatures exceeding 40°C. The department’s Daily Weather Report and Forecast Bulletin indicates that heatwave conditions are likely to persist for the next six days.
IMD reports that “heat wave to severe heat wave conditions prevailed in few pockets over East Uttar Pradesh & Vidarbha; Heat wave conditions in many pockets of Haryana, Chandigarh & Delhi and Himachal Pradesh; in some pockets of East Madhya Pradesh; in isolated pockets of Uttarakhand, Odisha, Punjab, West Uttar Pradesh & Coastal Andhra Pradesh.”
In response, Punjab Chief Minister Bhagwant Mann announced on the social media platform X that, starting Monday, May 25, government offices and schools across all classes will operate from 7:30 AM to 1:30 PM.
In Delhi, the Ridge station recorded a maximum temperature of 45.3°C, confirming a formal heatwave condition. Lodhi Road registered 44.3°C, with other stations such as Ayanagar at 44.5°C, Palam at 44.3°C, and Safdarjung at 43.6°C also reporting high temperatures.
The IMD has issued a severe heatwave warning for East Uttar Pradesh until May 28. Banda district recorded one of the highest temperatures in the country at 46.4°C. To protect the public during peak heat, traffic signals were deactivated during daytime hours to prevent commuters from waiting in direct sunlight. The district administration created temporary shelters at major intersections and public squares to provide immediate relief.
Additional Superintendent of Police Shivraj Prajapati said, “The district administration has constructed shelters at all major intersections and squares, offering a place of refuge during the scorching heat.”
Gujarat continues to face harsh weather, with temperatures between 42°C and 43°C in major urban centres. Rajkot Medical Officer of Health Jayesh L Vakani advised residents to avoid outdoor activities during the afternoon, particularly senior citizens and children. He stated, “The temperature in Rajkot has increased to 42-43 degrees…No one should go out in the afternoon without any important work…Senior citizens and children, especially, should avoid going out.”
Dehradun Meteorological Centre Director CS Tomar reported temperatures between 40°C and 42°C in Dehradun and Haridwar, describing the conditions as heatwave-like. He added that light to moderate rain is expected in northern districts such as Uttarkashi, Chamoli, Rudraprayag, Pithoragarh, and Bageshwar. He said, “There is a possibility of light to moderate rain at some places there.”
Dr Anoop Purkayastha, Consultant and Head of Emergency Medicine at Fortis Escorts Heart Institute in New Delhi, explained that heat indices can raise the perceived temperature during heatwaves, stating, “During a heatwave, when the temperature ranges from 42 to 43 degrees Celsius, it often feels closer to 45, 46, or even 48 degrees due to the heat index. In such conditions, a patient may experience symptoms like nausea, vomiting, confusion, severe dehydration, and extreme thirst. They might also experience fits or seizures. In medical terms, this condition is known as heat stroke. It is a critical medical emergency requiring rapid intervention to cool down the patient’s body temperature. Failing to do so can lead to severe and irreversible damage to vital organs, including the heart, brain, kidneys, and liver.”
Dr Ajay Chauhan, Director Professor of Medicine at RML Hospital, emphasised the serious risks associated with heatstroke, citing a high mortality rate. He said, “Since the beginning of this heatwave season, we have had two cases of full-fledged heatstroke admitted to our hospital…I would like to appeal to my fellow citizens to take adequate precautions during this severe weather because once a heatstroke occurs–although it is not a very common condition–it carries an exceptionally high mortality rate, with 60% or more of affected individuals losing their lives.”
